items.q: What happens in the recapitulation of sonata form?
items.correct: Both subjects return, but the second subject is now in the tonic key.
items.why: Students miss the KEY RESOLUTION. The exposition put subject 2 in a contrasting key; the recapitulation brings it back to the tonic, resolving the tonal tension - this is the dramatic point of sonata form.

items.q: What distinguishes Classical (1750-1820) from Baroque style?
items.correct: Clearer melodic phrases, homophonic texture, balanced structures like sonata form.
items.random_wrong: Classical means any old serious music.
items.why: Students use 'classical' loosely. The Classical period specifically reacted against Baroque complexity - simpler textures, clearer cadences, and balanced phrase structures (Mozart, Haydn, early Beethoven).

items.q: What is a balanced antecedent-consequent phrase?
items.correct: Two phrases of equal length where the first sounds open (question) and the second resolves (answer).
items.misconception: Any two phrases of similar style.
items.random_wrong: A long melody with no breaks.
items.why: Students miss the FUNCTIONAL relationship. The antecedent typically ends on an imperfect cadence (open); the consequent answers with a perfect cadence (closed). Length matches; cadences contrast.

items.q: Which 1969 Miles Davis album is foundational jazz fusion?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Bitches Brew. “Kind of Blue” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Which 1986 Paul Simon album featured South African musicians?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Graceland. “Hearts and Bones” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: In what year did Peter Gabriel found WOMAD?
items.why: Exact answer: 1982 AD. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
